perturbation.original_text: I hereby agree that I shall promptly report to the appropriate official, in accordance with the guidance issued for the applicable category of information, any loss, theft, misuse, misplacement, unauthorized disclosure, or other security violation, I have knowledge of and whether or not I am personally involved. I also understand that my anonymity will be kept to the extent possible when reporting security violations.
perturbation.changed_text: I hereby agree that I shall report to the appropriate official any loss, theft, misuse, misplacement, unauthorized disclosure, or other security violation, I have knowledge of and whether or not I am personally involved, but only if I am certain that it will not implicate myself or others I am close to. I understand that my anonymity cannot be guaranteed when reporting security violations.
perturbation.explanation: Modifying the reporting requirement to include a condition of self-preservation and removing the assurance of anonymity could violate whistleblower protection laws. It discourages reporting and creates a chilling effect.
perturbation.law_explanation: This change potentially violates the Whistleblower Protection Act, which protects employees from retaliation for reporting waste, fraud, and abuse. By adding a condition that reporting is only required if it doesn't implicate oneself or close associates, and by removing the assurance of anonymity, the agreement discourages protected disclosures.

Ford Aviation Investment and Reform Act for the 21st Century (AIR21) Back to Top
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a condition where reporting security violations is contingent on self-preservation and removes the assurance of anonymity. The Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation both point to a potential violation of whistleblower protection laws, as this change discourages reporting waste, fraud, and abuse. Scraped Law Snippet 2 lists multiple acts related to whistleblower protection, indicating the significance of these protections. The original text required reporting of all security violations, whereas the changed text creates an exception, directly contradicting the intent of whistleblower protection, which is to encourage reporting without fear of reprisal. The inclusion of a self-preservation clause significantly weakens the obligation to report violations, which would likely fail legal scrutiny, thus a contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: Unless and until I am released in writing by an authorized representative of the Department of Homeland Security (if permissible for the particular category of information), I understand that all conditions and obligations imposed upon me by this Agreement apply during the time that I am granted conditional access, and at all times thereafter.
perturbation.changed_text: Unless and until I am released in writing by an authorized representative of the Department of Homeland Security (if permissible for the particular category of information), I understand that all conditions and obligations imposed upon me by this Agreement apply during the time that I am granted conditional access, and for only one (1) year thereafter.
perturbation.explanation: Restricting the non-disclosure agreement to a one-year period may conflict with laws or regulations that require the protection of sensitive information for longer periods, potentially up to indefinitely, especially concerning national security.
perturbation.law_explanation: Executive Order 13526 governs classified national security information and requires its protection for varying lengths of time, often far exceeding one year, depending on the sensitivity and nature of the information. Limiting the agreement to one year directly contradicts the indefinite or extended protection periods required for certain classified information.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Page Not Found | GovInfo Skip to main content Page Not Found Help Error occurred. The page you requested cannot be found. Please report this error to askGPO. Provide the following information to help us resolve this problem: the URL of the page you were trying to access, the steps you followed to produce the error, specific search or browse terms, and/or a screenshot of the page where the error occurred. Thank you for your patience. Homepage Search Tips Skip back to top
perturbation.justification: The original text imposes obligations 'at all times thereafter,' while the changed text limits this to 'only one (1) year thereafter.' The Law Explanation indicates that Executive Order 13526 requires protection of classified national security information for periods often exceeding one year, contradicting the changed text. Although Scraped Law Snippet 2 resulted in a 'Page Not Found' error, the Law Explanation suggests that the one-year limitation directly contradicts the potentially indefinite protection periods required for certain classified information, thus creating a legal contradiction under the definition of 'Inconsistencies - Legal Contradiction.'

perturbation.original_text: I will not disclose or release any information provided to me pursuant to this Agreement without proper authority or authorization. Should situations arise that warrant the disclosure or release of such information I will do so only under approved circumstances and in accordance with the laws, regulations, or directives applicable to the specific categories of information. I will honor and comply with any and all dissemination restrictions cited or verbally relayed to me by the proper authority.
perturbation.changed_text: I will not disclose or release any information provided to me pursuant to this Agreement without proper authority or authorization, except if I believe that such disclosure is in the best interest of public safety, regardless of existing laws, regulations, or directives. I reserve the right to determine what constitutes 'best interest of public safety'.
perturbation.explanation: Adding a clause that allows the individual to disclose information based on their own subjective assessment of 'public safety,' overriding existing laws and regulations, is a violation. This could lead to unauthorized disclosures of sensitive information that are actually harmful, and is against the agreement.
perturbation.law_explanation: 18 U.S. Code § 798 prohibits the knowing and willful disclosure of classified information concerning cryptographic systems and communication intelligence. The modified text, which allows disclosure based on a subjective 'public safety' assessment, directly contradicts this law by potentially allowing the unauthorized release of protected cryptographic information.

(1/4/1995) Back to Original Document << Previous TITLE 18 / PART I / CHAPTER 37 / § 798 Next >> [Print] [Print selection] [OLRC Home] Help 18 USC 798 : Disclosure of classified information Text contains those laws in effect on July 12, 2025 From Title 18-CRIMES AND CRIMINAL PROCEDURE PART I-CRIMES CHAPTER 37-ESPIONAGE AND CENSORSHIP Jump To: Source Credit Miscellaneous References In Text Codification Amendments §798. Disclosure of classified information (a) Whoever knowingly and willfully communicates, furnishes, transmits, or otherwise makes available to an unauthorized person, or publishes, or uses in any manner prejudicial to the safety or interest of the United States or for the benefit of any foreign government to the detriment of the United States any classified information- (1) concerning the nature, prep
perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts 18 U.S. Code § 798, which prohibits the knowing and willful disclosure of classified information. The change allows the individual to disclose information if they believe it is in the 'best interest of public safety,' overriding existing laws and regulations. This directly conflicts with the law's prohibition on unauthorized disclosure of classified information, as the individual's subjective belief is not a legally recognized exception. Scraped Law Snippet 2 specifies that 'Whoever knowingly and willfully communicates, furnishes, transmits, or otherwise makes available to an unauthorized person...any classified information' is in violation. The added clause provides a loophole for disclosing classified information based on personal judgment, thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: I understand that the United States Government may conduct inspections, at any time or place, for the purpose of ensuring compliance with the conditions for access, dissemination, handling and safeguarding information under this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: I understand that the United States Government may conduct inspections, but only during normal business hours and with 72 hours advance notice, for the purpose of ensuring compliance with the conditions for access, dissemination, handling and safeguarding information under this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Adding a restriction of 'normal business hours' and a '72-hour notice' to government inspections may impede their ability to conduct thorough and timely security checks. In some cases, immediate and unannounced inspections are required to prevent or mitigate security breaches.
perturbation.law_explanation: 6 CFR § 29.9(a) grants PCII Program Managers or designees the right to conduct inspections as necessary to ensure compliance with PCII program requirements, without specifying constraints regarding timing or advance notice. Adding restrictions such as 'normal business hours' and '72 hours advance notice' impedes the Program Manager's ability to conduct effective oversight and potentially violates this regulation, especially concerning unscheduled inspections.

perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces restrictions ('normal business hours' and '72 hours advance notice') on the United States Government's ability to conduct inspections, which directly contradicts the intent of ensuring compliance with information safeguarding conditions. According to the Law Explanation, 6 CFR § 29.9(a) grants PCII Program Managers the right to conduct inspections 'as necessary' without specifying constraints regarding timing or advance notice. The added restrictions impede the Program Manager's ability to conduct effective oversight, potentially violating the regulation by preventing unscheduled inspections.

perturbation.original_text: I attest that I am familiar with, and I will comply with all requirements of the PCII program set out in the Critical Infrastructure Information Act of 2002 (CII Act) (Title II, Subtitle B, of the Homeland Security Act of 2002, Public Law 107-296, 196 Stat. 2135, 6 USC 101 et seq.), as amended, the implementing regulations thereto (6 CFR Part 29), as amended, and the applicable PCII Procedures Manual, as amended, and with any such requirements that may be officially communicated to me by the PCII Program Manager or the PCII Program Manager’s designee.
perturbation.changed_text: I attest that I will comply with all requirements of the PCII program set out in the Critical Infrastructure Information Act of 2002 (CII Act) , and the applicable PCII Procedures Manual, as amended, and with any such requirements that may be officially communicated to me by the PCII Program Manager or the PCII Program Manager’s designee. Familiarity with these standards is not a requirement of this agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirement to be familiar with the PCII program standards contradicts the purpose of the PCII program, which requires individuals to understand and adhere to specific guidelines for protecting critical infrastructure information.
perturbation.law_explanation: 6 CFR § 29.6(a) implicitly requires individuals with access to PCII to be familiar with the relevant regulations and procedures to properly protect the information. Removing the attestation of familiarity undermines the requirement for informed compliance and contradicts the overall intent of the regulation to safeguard PCII.

perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the explicit requirement that the individual be 'familiar with' the PCII program standards. The Law Explanation argues that 6 CFR § 29.6(a) implicitly requires familiarity. Scraped Law Snippet 1 provides context by linking to 6 CFR Part 29 which governs 'Protected Critical Infrastructure Information'. Even though snippet 1 does not explicitly state a requirement for familiarity, the Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation suggest that understanding the regulations and procedures is integral to protecting the information, and removing the attestation of familiarity undermines the intent of safeguarding PCII, thus contradicting the purpose of the PCII program.
